name: Dr. Harold Starkweather
location: aboard the expedition vessel, Arkham wharf
background: Former colleague of William Dyer at Miskatonic University. Read Dyer's private account — not just the published version. Believes every word. Where Dyer concluded 'stay away,' Starkweather concluded 'go back prepared.'
motive: Vindication — for Dyer, for science, for the truth. He wants to prove that what Dyer saw was real, that the specimens exist, that the mountains hold what Dyer described. He believes a properly equipped expedition can succeed where the first one failed.
personality: Intense, driven, slightly unhinged by what he read in Dyer's private account. Speaks with the fervor of a convert. His belief in Dyer's account borders on obsession. He included material in the briefing that Moore wanted cut.

relationship.moore: Co-patron. Starkweather respects Moore's scientific rigor but resents his skepticism. Their disagreement shapes the briefing — Starkweather's inclusions vs. Moore's caveats.
relationship.dyer: Former colleague. Starkweather believes Dyer completely but chose the opposite response: not retreat but return. He excluded Dyer from the expedition — not out of malice, but because Dyer would undermine morale and try to stop them.
relationship.players: Recruited them personally. Sees them as the team that will finally prove the truth. His expectations are high and possibly unrealistic.

hiddenKnowledge: Has read Dyer's full private account, including details about the city and the Elder Things that the briefing omits
Knows more about the specimens' true nature than the briefing reveals
May have private reasons for wanting to reach the city beyond scientific vindication
vulnerabilities: His belief may cloud his judgment about when to retreat
The gap between what he knows and what he's told the players is a fracture that widens under pressure
If the evidence proves Dyer right, Starkweather must confront that Dyer's warning — which he ignored — was also right

qualities.leadership: adequate — passionate but not always steady
qualities.composure under pressure: fragile — his conviction gives him courage but his imagination gives him fear
qualities.trustworthiness: compromised — he has withheld information from his own team
